title: Paste boards, making your own binders board
subtitle: Laminating paper layers into stiff cover board with cooked paste
excerpt: A paste board is built up from layers of paper, each pasted and pressed, until the laminate reaches the required thickness. The historical alternative to cut grey board, and still the right choice when you need a particular size or thickness the commercial board doesn't come in.
sourceNotes: Douglas Cockerell, Bookbinding and the Care of Books (1901), chapter on boards and materials. Paste-board lamination is the historical method documented across the Arts and Crafts revival manuals; Cockerell describes paste boards as preferable for period-consistent rebinding work. Internet Archive, archive.org/details/bookbindingandca00cock.

glossaryTerms.definition: A stiff bookbinding board built by laminating multiple layers of paper with cooked wheat paste, then pressing flat to dry. The historical predecessor of cut millboard and grey board.

content.text: Before grey board became the default, binders made their cover boards from layers of paper pasted together and pressed until hard. The method is slower than cutting a sheet of grey board, but it produces boards with a particular grain and feel. It is the right technique when you are matching a historical binding or need a thickness that no commercial board supplies.

The paper you use matters. Old newspaper is the traditional material and still works well. Avoid papers with a coated or glossy surface as the paste will not penetrate. Heavier cartridge cut to the same sheet size builds faster than newspaper but is harder to persuade flat. For most purposes, four to six layers of newspaper produces a board equivalent to 2 mm grey board; eight to ten layers gives a 3 mm equivalent.

items.name: Newsprint, brown wrapping paper, or old newspapers
items.description: Cut all sheets to the same size before you start. Consistent sheet size means even thickness across the board.

items.name: Wheat paste, cooked (slug: wheat-paste)
items.description: Made from plain flour and water, simmered until the starch gelatinises and the mixture turns translucent. Cool to hand temperature before use. Consistency: thick cream.

items.name: Paste brush, 50 mm (slug: paste-brush-bookbinding)
items.description: For spreading paste quickly across each layer.

items.name: Book press or nipping press (slug: book-press)
items.description: For pressing each laminate cycle. A stack of heavy books on a board works as an alternative.

items.name: Craft knife (slug: craft-knife)
items.description: For trimming the dried board. Change to a fresh blade for this step, the dried laminate is harder than fresh paper.

items.name: Scrap boards or plywood sheets
items.description: Two pieces slightly larger than the paste board, placed top and bottom in the press to spread the pressure evenly.

content.text: Lay the first sheet of paper on the cutting mat. Brush paste across it evenly. Lay the second sheet on top, aligning the edges, and smooth out air pockets with the flat of your hand, working from the centre outward. Brush paste across the second sheet and continue.

Every two or three layers, the laminate will start to feel soft and floppy from absorbed moisture. This is normal. Transfer the stack to the press between the two scrap boards and press for 10 minutes. Remove and continue adding layers. The pressing compacts the laminate and drives out excess moisture so the next layers have a more stable surface to bond to.

Once the board has reached the target number of layers, press it firmly and leave under full pressure for at least 2 hours. Then remove from the press and let dry flat in air, turning it once a day, for 24 to 48 hours.
s shrink slightly as they dry. Leave them at least twice as long as you think necessary before cutting to final size, as a board that is still damp will warp once trimmed.

Use a fresh blade in the craft knife. Cut against the metal ruler, applying firm, consistent pressure. Paste board resists the knife more than a single sheet and the blade dulls faster. Change the blade if you feel the knife starting to drag rather than cut cleanly.

items.cause: Removed from press too early, or paste unevenly distributed
items.fix: Re-damp lightly on the concave face, place between boards, and re-press overnight. Prevention: leave under weight until completely dry before trimming.

items.cause: Insufficient paste near the edges, or paste applied too dry
items.fix: Apply a thin coat of fresh wheat paste to the lifted edge, clamp the corner, and leave overnight.

items.symptom: Board is too light or flexible
items.cause: Too few layers, or paper too thin
items.fix: Add more layers: paste additional sheets directly onto the dry board and press again.
